description: Non-Hawaii residents must reserve parking and entry to Hāʻena State Park in advance. This covers access to Ke'e Beach, Tunnels Reef, and the Kalalau Trail trailhead.

seasonalWindow.notes: Reservations release 30 days in advance at midnight HST and sell out quickly for peak dates. Walk-in shuttle access may be available from Wainiha — check the website.

tips: Book exactly 30 days ahead at midnight Hawaii time for best availability.
Hawaii residents with valid ID can enter without a reservation.
The reservation covers parking, not a separate entry fee.
Arrive early — the lot fills and late arrivals may be turned away even with a reservation.

activity: Nā Pali Coast Camping Permit (Kalalau Trail)
description: Overnight camping along the Kalalau Trail (beyond Hanakāpīʻai Beach) requires a camping permit from the Hawaii Division of State Parks. Permits cover Hanakoa Valley and Kalalau Beach campsites.

seasonalWindow.notes: Permits release exactly 90 days in advance and sell out within minutes for summer dates. Set a calendar reminder.

tips: Permits release at midnight HST, 90 days before the start date.
Summer weekends sell out in under 5 minutes — be ready to click.
Plan at least 2 nights to avoid rushing the 11-mile trail.
The trail is slippery year-round. Trekking poles and waterproof bags are essential.
No fires allowed at Kalalau Beach — bring a camp stove.

description: Camping at Kauaʻi state parks (Polihale, Kōkeʻe, Haena) requires advance reservations through the Hawaii State Parks system.
cost: $12–$30 per night depending on park and site type

seasonalWindow.notes: Polihale access road may be impassable in wet conditions — check ahead. Kōkeʻe cabins book separately through kokee.net.

tips: Polihale requires a high-clearance vehicle — the 5-mile dirt road is rough.
Kōkeʻe cabins are an alternative to tent camping at elevation — book at kokee.net.
Bring all supplies to Polihale — there are no services for miles.
Check the state parks website for current closures before heading out.

description: Hawaii law bans sunscreens containing oxybenzone and octinoxate. Reef-safe sunscreen is required at all beaches and snorkel sites. No separate permit is needed for snorkeling, but Hāʻena State Park (Tunnels Reef, Ke'e Beach) requires an entry reservation.

seasonalWindow.notes: Enforced at all state beaches. Non-compliant sunscreens may be confiscated at park entrances.

tips: Buy reef-safe sunscreen before arriving — it's cheaper on the mainland.
Mineral-based sunscreens (zinc oxide, titanium dioxide) are the safest choice.
Rash guards reduce sunscreen needs and provide better protection in the water.
The law applies to all beaches in Hawaii, not just state parks.
